EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed across the Northern Plains Wednesday evening, quickly moved across Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning of Thursday, September 4th. Several reports of downed trees and power lines from Rice Lake to Ladysmith occurred as these storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in the city of Rice Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning of September 20th across western Wisconsin ahead of a warm front that was moving east across the region. Some of these storms produced large hail with penny sized near West Salem (La Crosse County) the largest reported.

Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across western Wisconsin on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County). All the schools across western Wisconsin were closed on both January 27th and 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Monroe County from the morning of January 27th into the morning of the 28th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 35 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Sparta airport and a mesonet reporting station near Tomah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms developed across west central Wisconsin during the late afternoon of Thursday, July 31st. One of the storms produced quarter size hail.
The first days of March remained extremely cold, with wind chill values dropping to -35F or colder for several hours during the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of surface observations indicated that temperatures and wind speeds were cold enough to produced wind chill values of -35F or colder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms developed across west central Wisconsin during the late afternoon of Thursday, July 31st. One of the storms produced quarter size hail.

North to south bands of heavy snow moved east through southern Wisconsin along a cold front from the morning into the early afternoon. The sudden reduction in visibility of less than 1/4 mile and quick snow accumulation of 1-2 inches resulted in numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent heavy snow resulting in a quick 1-2 inches, and sudden visibility reductions less than 1/4 mile. Hazardous travel was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North to south bands of heavy snow moved east through southern Wisconsin along a cold front from the morning into the early afternoon. The sudden reduction in visibility of less than 1/4 mile and quick snow accumulation of 1-2 inches resulted in numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent heavy snow resulting in a quick 1-2 inches, and sudden visibility reductions less than 1/4 mile. Numerous accidents involving 17 vehicles occurred on highway 151 between mile markers 108-111. The highway was closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with cold air aloft to produce scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms across the area.  One of the storms dropped large hail in the Fox Valley as it passed through Little Chute and Kimberly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail as it passed through Little Chute and Kimberly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a northward advancing warm front during the afternoon of June 27th. Some of these storms produced high winds that knocked down large tree branches and power lines from Longwood (Clark County) north to near Medford and Gilman (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown west of Medford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong and slow moving thunderstorms moved across southern WI during the morning hours due to the passage of a warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few flooded streets or standing water on streets in the low lying areas of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed across the Northern Plains Wednesday evening, quickly moved across Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning of Thursday, September 4th. Several reports of downed trees and power lines from Rice Lake to Ladysmith occurred as these storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in the city of Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped out of central Canada and moved across the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes Region on January 14th. One person was injured in one of several crashes on Interstate 94 near Osseo (Trempealeau County). These crashes involved about 20 semis and shut down the westbound lanes of the interstate for several hours. The storm system covered the region with a fresh blanket of snow with many locations receiving between 6 and 9 inches. The highest reported total was 9.2 inches in Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 2 and 7 inches of snow across the county. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches near Camp Douglas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 2 and 8 inches of snow across Monroe County. The highest reported total was 7.5 inches in Tomah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm air aloft and an upper level front brought several inches of powdery snow to portions of south central Wisconsin, including Madison and locations to the  south and west of Madison. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during the morning rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of powdery snow fell overnight and into the morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme cold wave of arctic air and brisk winds brought 40 below wind chills to southern Wisconsin. Numerous school and business closings occurred. This cold wave and the continued cold through January, resulted in numerous water main breaks, especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Thousands of car batteries were replaced during the month. The Wisconsin Commissioner of Railroads cited cracked rails due to the cold conditions that caused the derailment of 19 cars on a Union Pacific coal train in Caledonia at 6 am on January 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures reached a minimum in the -40s Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure that tracked from the Central Plains into Lower Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow and windy conditions, resulting in blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities. Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow. Heavy snow with reduced visibilities less than 1/4 mile reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow, freezing drizzle, and gusty southwest winds developed within a warm advection regime ahead of an approaching cold front during the morning into the afternoon. Another area of light snow developed in the evening with the passage of the cold front. Peak wind gusts of 35-50 mph and a powdery snow pack resulted in blowing and drifting snow, and reduced visibilities less than a mile at times, especially in open areas. Multiple vehicle slide-offs and accidents were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 30-40 mph resulting in blowing and drifting snow, and reduced visibilities to less than a mile at times in open areas. 0.5-1 inch of snow, and light freezing drizzle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40-50 mph resulting in blowing and drifting snow, and reduced visibilities to less than a mile at times in open areas. 1-2 inches of snow, and light freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively warm air aloft and an upper level front brought a few inches of powdery snow to portions of southern Wisconsin during the morning rush hour. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred mainly along the Interstate 94 corridor. The poor road conditions were a contributing factor in a fatal accident in Kenosha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of powdery snow during the morning rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through southern Wisconsin from the late afternoon through the tonight period. West to northwest winds gusted to 35-50 mph with blizzard conditions mainly south and west of Madison. Light snow accompanied the cold front but the blowing and drifting of a powdery snow pack had the biggest impact on visibility and road conditions. Many vehicle slide-offs and stranded motorists were reported in the counties south and west of Madison, due to whiteout conditions and deep snow drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest wind gusts of 35-40 mph, light snow, and blowing and drifting snow were reported. Travel was hazardous across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking east across far southern Wisconsin resulted in several inches of snow and blowing snow across the region. The combination of snow and brisk winds were the greatest mainly north of Madison and Milwaukee, where freezing rain also occurred in the afternoon. Less intense snow and winds occurred from Madison to Milwaukee and south to the Illinois border. The snow covered and icy roads resulted in many vehicle slide-offs and accidents, especially north of Madison and Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to five inches of snow, blowing and drifting snow, and a coating of ice due to freezing rain resulted in numerous slide-offs and accidents. A section of I-39/90 northbound was closed for a time near hwy 60, mainly due to the freezing rain. Wind gusts were mainly in the 30 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow, freezing drizzle, and gusty southwest winds developed within a warm advection regime ahead of an approaching cold front during the morning into the afternoon. Multiple vehicle slide-offs and accidents were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking east across far southern Wisconsin resulted in several inches of snow and blowing snow across the region. The combination of snow and brisk winds were the greatest mainly north of Madison and Milwaukee, where freezing rain also occurred in the afternoon. Less intense snow and winds occurred from Madison to Milwaukee and south to the Illinois border. The snow covered and icy roads resulted in many vehicle slide-offs and accidents, especially north of Madison and Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow, and blowing and drifting snow resulted in numerous slide-offs and accidents. Wind gusts were mainly in the 30 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold wave affected southern Wisconsin during this period. West to northwest winds of 10-20 mph with the passage of an arctic cold front brought wind chill temperatures of 20 below to 38 below zero Fahrenheit beginning the early morning of January 27th, and not ending until the morning of January 29th. The coldest period was the morning of January 28th when wind chills ranged from 30 below to 38 below zero. Widespread school and business closings occurred during this time. The Governor declared a state of emergency due to a propane shortage across the state. Numerous water main breaks and frozen laterals continued to occur throughout the entire month of January. Two cold weather deaths occurred, one in Rock County and one in Milwaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 20 below to 34 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure, which originated in Canada, intensified as it moved across Wisconsin.  A swath of 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across central and northeast Wisconsin.  As the low intensified, north winds increased and gusted over 35 mph at times across the Fox Valley and lakeshore, causing blowing and drifting snow.||The highest snowfall totals included 15.0 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.), 13.0 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.), 12.2 inches at Egg Harbor (Door Co.), 11.0 inches at Oconto (Southern Oconto Co.) and Juddville (Door Co.), 10.5 inches at Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), 10.0 inches near Beaver (Southern Marinette Co.), 9.4 inches at Plover (Portage Co.) and 9.0 inches at Peshtigo (Southern Marinette Co.). A snowfall of 7.5 inches was recorded in Green Bay.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 8.0 inches of snow fell at Wild Rose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme cold wave of arctic air and brisk winds brought 40 below wind chills to southern Wisconsin. Numerous school and business closings occurred. This cold wave and the continued cold through January, resulted in numerous water main breaks, especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Thousands of car batteries were replaced during the month. The Wisconsin Commissioner of Railroads cited cracked rails due to the cold conditions that caused the derailment of 19 cars on a Union Pacific coal train in Caledonia at 6 am on January 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures reached a minimum in the -40s Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across western Wisconsin from the evening of January 5th into the late evening of the 6th or early morning of the 7th. The coldest wind chills of 40 to 55 below occurred on the morning of the 6th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 4th and 5th which dropped the air temperatures well below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 53 below from a mesonet reporting station in Friendship (Adams County). Almost all the schools across western Wisconsin closed January 6th and most of the schools were closed again on the 7th or delayed the start of classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Adams County from the early morning of January 6th into the early morning of the 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 53 below from a mesonet reporting station in Friendship.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly woman suffering from Dementia fell in her apartment with the heat turned off on February 5th. She was found that same day and taken to the hospital. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported she died in the hospital of complications from hypothermia on February 6th. Outside air temperatures ranged from the teens to lower 20s Fahrenheit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent heavy snow resulting in a quick 1-2 inches, and sudden visibility reductions less than 1/4 mile. Hazardous travel was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow with multiple slide-offs and accidents. The snow began the afternoon of December 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure that tracked from the Central Plains into Lower Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow and windy conditions, resulting in blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities. Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5-7 inches with southeast wind gusts of 30-40 mph. Heavy snow and blowing snow with visibilities less than 1/4 mile was reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest Wisconsin was hit by a blizzard during the afternoon and evening of January 26th. Sustained winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to 45 mph produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur making many roads impassable. During the blizzard, snow plows were pulled off the roads in Grant County. The stage was set for the blizzard to occur when a system passed across the region during the evening of the 25th and early morning of 26th and produced 2 to 3 inches of light fluffy snow. This snow then was used by the winds, along with additional falling light snow, to create the blizzard conditions behind a strong area of low pressure that moved across central Minnesota and northern Wisconsin. The highest measured wind gust was 45 mph from the automated weather observing equipment at the Boscobel (Grant County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds between 25 and 40 mph with gusts to 45 mph, created a period of blizzard conditions across Grant County. As a result of the blizzard, numerous roads were impassable due to severe drifting with some vehicles becoming trapped in the snow. All schools in the county were closed on the 27th from a combination of the conditions left behind by the blizzard and extremely cold wind chills. The highest measured wind gust during the blizzard was 45 mph from the automated weather observing equipment at the Boscobel airport.

Temperatures fell to lows in the 16 below to 32 below zero range.  The cold temperatures, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills in the 40 below to 55 below zero range.  The coldest temperatures and wind chills were generally across central and north central Wisconsin.

The coldest wind chills of 40 to 55 below occurred on the morning of the 6th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 4th and 5th which dropped the air temperatures well below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 53 below from a mesonet reporting station in Friendship (Adams County). Almost all the schools across western Wisconsin closed January 6th and most of the schools were closed again on the 7th or delayed the start of classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Jackson County from the early morning of January 6th into the early morning of the 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 48 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme cold wave of arctic air and brisk winds brought 40 below wind chills to southern Wisconsin. Numerous school and business closings occurred. This cold wave and the continued cold through January, resulted in numerous water main breaks, especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Thousands of car batteries were replaced during the month. The Wisconsin Commissioner of Railroads cited cracked rails due to the cold conditions that caused the derailment of 19 cars on a Union Pacific coal train in Caledonia at 6 am on January 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures reached a minimum in the -40s Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: No changes occurred during January, 2014 to the severe drought across western Wisconsin. Because the ground was frozen, no moisture was able to work into the soil from the 15 to 20 inches of snow that fell. As a result, the severe drought continued across the northern portions of Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties into the northwest corner of Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No changes occurred to the severe drought across the northwest corner of Jackson County. Because the ground was frozen, no moisture was able to soak into the soil from the 15 to 20 inches of snow that fell.

The coldest period was early Monday. Most schools across northwest Wisconsin closed for both Monday and Tuesday.

Low pressure, which originated in Canada, intensified as it moved across Wisconsin.  A swath of 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across central and northeast Wisconsin.  As the low intensified, north winds increased and gusted over 35 mph at times across the Fox Valley and lakeshore, causing blowing and drifting snow.||The highest snowfall totals included 15.0 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.), 13.0 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.), 12.2 inches at Egg Harbor (Door Co.), 11.0 inches at Oconto (Southern Oconto Co.) and Juddville (Door Co.), 10.5 inches at Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), 10.0 inches near Beaver (Southern Marinette Co.), 9.4 inches at Plover (Portage Co.) and 9.0 inches at Peshtigo (Southern Marinette Co.). A snowfall of 7.5 inches was recorded in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 7.0 inches of snow fell in New London.

Lake effect snow occurred during and after this event with higher snow totals over far southeast Wisconsin. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to six inches of snow with multiple slide-offs and accidents. The snow began the afternoon of December 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, some contributed by the effect of lake Superior, fell over a long duration over parts of northwest Wisconsin. Parts of northern Bayfield County, northeastern Ashland County, and northern Iron County had 8 to 12 inches of snowfall.  Reports included 11 at Cornucopia, 10 in Saxon, 9.3 inches at Gile, 9 inches at Hurley, 8 inches 8 miles north of Bayfield and 7.5 in Gurney.

Southwest Wisconsin was hit by a blizzard during the afternoon and evening of January 26th. Sustained winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts to 45 mph produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur making many roads impassable. During the blizzard, snow plows were pulled off the roads in Grant County. The stage was set for the blizzard to occur when a system passed across the region during the evening of the 25th and early morning of 26th and produced 2 to 3 inches of light fluffy snow. This snow then was used by the winds, along with additional falling light snow, to create the blizzard conditions behind a strong area of low pressure that moved across central Minnesota and northern Wisconsin. The highest measured wind gust was 45 mph from the automated weather observing equipment at the Boscobel (Grant County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, created considerable blowing and drifting snow across Crawford County after 2 to 3 inches of snow fell. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions. All the schools across the county were closed on the 27th as a result of the conditions produced by the blowing and drifting snow and extremely cold wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Plains and upper level flow from the Canadian Plains brought extremely cold temperatures and wind chills to the area.  Temperatures fell to lows in the 15 below to 28 below zero range.  West winds of 10 to 20 mph combined with the frigid air to produce wind chills in the 35 below to 45 below zero range, and as low as 48 below zero at Rhinelander.  The coldest temperatures and wind chills were generally across central and north central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest temperature recorded in Southern Marinette County was 28 degrees below zero at Peshtigo. Wind chills were as low as 34 below zero.

Intensifying low pressure that tracked from the Central Plains into Lower Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow and windy conditions, resulting in blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities. Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were around 5 inches with southeast wind gusts of 30-40 mph. Heavy snow and blowing snow with visibilities less than 1/4 mile was reported at times.

Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow. Heavy snow with reduced visibilities less than 1/4 mile reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow, freezing drizzle, and gusty southwest winds developed within a warm advection regime ahead of an approaching cold front during the morning into the afternoon. Multiple vehicle slide-offs and accidents were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a surge of warm air aloft over a frozen ground resulted in widespread freezing rain over southern Wisconsin. Ice accumulations mostly under 1/4 inch resulted in widespread travel problems. Hundreds of vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during the evening commute. Some hills became impassible due to the icy conditions, and some roads were temporarily closed to clean-up the multi-vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 0.05 to 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure, which originated in Canada, intensified as it moved across Wisconsin.  A swath of 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across central and northeast Wisconsin.  As the low intensified, north winds increased and gusted over 35 mph at times across the Fox Valley and lakeshore, causing blowing and drifting snow.||The highest snowfall totals included 15.0 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.), 13.0 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.), 12.2 inches at Egg Harbor (Door Co.), 11.0 inches at Oconto (Southern Oconto Co.) and Juddville (Door Co.), 10.5 inches at Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), 10.0 inches near Beaver (Southern Marinette Co.), 9.4 inches at Plover (Portage Co.) and 9.0 inches at Peshtigo (Southern Marinette Co.). A snowfall of 7.5 inches was recorded in Green Bay.

A bitterly cold arctic air mass, the coldest to impact the region in years, spread across the area following the passage of a cold front.  Temperatures fell to lows in the 16 below to 32 below zero range.  The cold temperatures, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills in the 40 below to 55 below zero range.  The coldest temperatures and wind chills were generally across central and north central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded temperature in Oneida County was 30 degrees below zero at Rhinelander.  Wind chills were as low as 55 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate ice jams along the Wolf River about 0.75 mile south of Keshena lead to flooding in the Keshena area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, comprised of frazil ice, caused low-land flooding along the Wolf River in Keshena.  An excavator was used in an attempt to open up a channel that would allow water to bypass the jam, but those efforts were unsuccessful.  About a dozen structures were affected by the high waters, and some homes had flooding in their basements.  The intersection of State Highways 47/55 and County Trunk VV East had to be closed for several days after the river began to spill over the road.  Work crews placed barriers along the Wolf River and constructed a dam in a small creek leading into the river.  They then used industrial electric pumps, capable of pumping about 500 gallons per minute, to remove water from the creek and pump it into the Wolf River.  This caused the flood waters to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across western Wisconsin from the evening of January 5th into the late evening of the 6th or early morning of the 7th. The coldest wind chills of 40 to 55 below occurred on the morning of the 6th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 4th and 5th which dropped the air temperatures well below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 53 below from a mesonet reporting station in Friendship (Adams County). Almost all the schools across western Wisconsin closed January 6th and most of the schools were closed again on the 7th or delayed the start of classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Taylor County from the evening of January 5th into the morning of the 7th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 51 below from a mesonet reporting station near Lublin and the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air and winds of 5 to 10 mph resulted in wind chill temperatures ranging mainly from 15 below to 30 below zero across southern Wisconsin. The Wisconsin Department of Health Services reported two probable cold-related deaths, one in Milwaukee County and one in Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of 10 below to 19 below zero prevailed during the morning. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported a probable cold weather death of a man who was found outside his home in his wheelchair in the city of Milwaukee. He was only partially clothed, and also suffered from Schizophrenia. It is believed he left his home sometime between 11 pm on January 2nd to 6 am on January 3rd. He was pronounced dead at 7:45 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air and winds of 5 to 10 mph resulted in wind chill temperatures ranging mainly from 15 below to 30 below zero across southern Wisconsin. The Wisconsin Department of Health Services reported two probable cold-related deaths, one in Milwaukee County and one in Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind Chill temperatures ranged from 20 below to 29 below zero during the morning hours. The Marquette County Coroner pronounced a disabled woman dead of hypothermia at 9:30 am. She was found in her driveway, and appeared to have fallen and was not able to stand back up. Her cane was found about 10 feet away from her. She would have fallen anytime from the evening of January 2nd to the morning of January 3rd.  Her home was located near Lawrence Lake, just west of Westfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across western Wisconsin on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County). All the schools across western Wisconsin were closed on both January 27th and 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Juneau County from the morning of January 27th into the morning of the 28th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 38 below from the automated weather observing equipment at Volk Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed in central Wisconsin as warm and moist air passed over much colder ground.  The dense fog contributed to a head-on collision on Highway 29 in Wittenberg, near the Grand Avenue overpass, around 4:30 p.m. CST.  A westbound truck crossed the median and collided with an eastbound minivan injuring 7 people (indirect), all of whom were treated at an area hospital and released later that same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog contributed to a head-on collision on Highway 29 in Wittenberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through southern Wisconsin from the late afternoon through the tonight period. West to northwest winds gusted to 35-50 mph with blizzard conditions mainly south and west of Madison. Light snow accompanied the cold front but the blowing and drifting of a powdery snow pack had the biggest impact on visibility and road conditions. Many vehicle slide-offs and stranded motorists were reported in the counties south and west of Madison, due to whiteout conditions and deep snow drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest wind gusts of 40-45 mph, light snow, and significant blowing and drifting snow was reported. This caused nearly whiteout conditions at times, especially over southwest Dane County. One road was closed due to deep snow drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 5 inches, with a few 6 to 7 inch amounts, were reported in far Southeast Wisconsin as the northern edge of a large snow shield to the north of surface low pressure moving up the Ohio River Valley reached southern Wisconsin. The highest amounts in far eastern Racine and Kenosha Counties were due to a band of lake-effect snow that lingered over the area after the main system snow had ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals mainly ranged from 3-5 inches, however lake effect snow resulted in a couple 7 inch reports near Lake Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped out of central Canada and moved across the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes Region on January 14th. One person was injured in one of several crashes on Interstate 94 near Osseo (Trempealeau County). These crashes involved about 20 semis and shut down the westbound lanes of the interstate for several hours. The storm system covered the region with a fresh blanket of snow with many locations receiving between 6 and 9 inches. The highest reported total was 9.2 inches in Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 4 and 9 inches of snow across Adams County. The highest reported total was 9.2 inches in Friendship.

As the low intensified, north winds increased and gusted over 35 mph at times across the Fox Valley and lakeshore, causing blowing and drifting snow.||The highest snowfall totals included 15.0 inches near Ephraim (Door Co.), 13.0 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.), 12.2 inches at Egg Harbor (Door Co.), 11.0 inches at Oconto (Southern Oconto Co.) and Juddville (Door Co.), 10.5 inches at Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), 10.0 inches near Beaver (Southern Marinette Co.), 9.4 inches at Plover (Portage Co.) and 9.0 inches at Peshtigo (Southern Marinette Co.). A snowfall of 7.5 inches was recorded in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many of the highest snowfall totals from the storm were recorded in Door County.  A total of 15.0 inches of snow fell at Ephraim, 12.2 inches fell at Egg Harbor, 11.0 inches fell at Juddville, 8.5 inches fell at Baileys Harbor and Forestville, and 7.4 inches fell at Washington Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure that tracked from the Central Plains into Lower Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow and windy conditions, resulting in blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities. Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow. Heavy snow with reduced visibilities less than 1/4 mile reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a surge of warm air aloft over a frozen ground resulted in widespread freezing rain over southern Wisconsin. Ice accumulations mostly under 1/4 inch resulted in widespread travel problems. Hundreds of vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during the evening commute. Some hills became impassible due to the icy conditions, and some roads were temporarily closed to clean-up the multi-vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 0.05 to 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow, freezing drizzle, and gusty southwest winds developed within a warm advection regime ahead of an approaching cold front during the morning into the afternoon. Another area of light snow developed in the evening with the passage of the cold front. Peak wind gusts of 35-50 mph and a powdery snow pack resulted in blowing and drifting snow, and reduced visibilities less than a mile at times, especially in open areas. Multiple vehicle slide-offs and accidents were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 45-51 mph resulting in blowing and drifting snow, and reduced visibilities to less than a mile at times in open areas. 0.5-2 inches of snow, and light freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air and 5 to 10 mph winds resulted in minimum wind chill temperatures around 20 below zero Fahrenheit for several hours during the morning. One death at least partially due to hypothermia occurred in Beaver Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was found dead on a frozen retention pond around 1 pm. His vehicle was running nearby, and he was not wearing a coat. Hypothermia is believed to have played a significant role in his death. Wind chill temperatures were around 20 below zero in the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two cars of a 100-car grain train derailed in downtown Janesville. A railroad switch malfunctioned due to the cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two cars of a 100-car grain train derailed in downtown Janesville about 5:30 am. A railroad switch malfunctioned due to the cold temperatures, which ranged from zero to 5 degrees Fahrenheit during the early morning hours. The two cars remained upright and were put back on the tracks. Court Street at the Five Points intersection was closed for about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped out of central Canada and moved across the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes Region on January 14th. One person was injured in one of several crashes on Interstate 94 near Osseo (Trempealeau County). These crashes involved about 20 semis and shut down the westbound lanes of the interstate for several hours. The storm system covered the region with a fresh blanket of snow with many locations receiving between 6 and 9 inches. The highest reported total was 9.2 inches in Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 4 and 8 inches of snow across Clark County. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Neillsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold arctic air mass, the coldest to impact the region in years, spread across the area following the passage of a cold front.  Temperatures fell to lows in the 16 below to 32 below zero range.  The cold temperatures, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills in the 40 below to 55 below zero range.  The coldest temperatures and wind chills were generally across central and north central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded temperature in Waupaca County was 23 degrees below zero at Waupaca. Wind chills were as low as 44 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded temperature in Waushara County was 22 degrees below zero at Wautoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through southern Wisconsin from the late afternoon through the tonight period. West to northwest winds gusted to 35-50 mph with blizzard conditions mainly south and west of Madison. Light snow accompanied the cold front but the blowing and drifting of a powdery snow pack had the biggest impact on visibility and road conditions. Many vehicle slide-offs and stranded motorists were reported in the counties south and west of Madison, due to whiteout conditions and deep snow drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest wind gusts of 35-45 mph, light snow, and blowing and drifting snow were reported. This caused hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively warm air aloft and an upper level front brought a few inches of powdery snow to portions of southern Wisconsin during the morning rush hour. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred mainly along the Interstate 94 corridor. The poor road conditions were a contributing factor in a fatal accident in Kenosha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 inches of powdery snow during the morning rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moved from central Nebraska and through Wisconsin. The low brought widespread snowfall of 8 to 16 inches to northwest Wisconsin with higher amounts in the snowbelt areas.  Off-lake winds provided lake-induced snow to the snowbelt areas. The snow tapered off for some areas in the afternoon and evening of the 11th, but winds blowing over the Lake resulted in lingering snow through the 12th and 13th.  Snowfall totals included 49 inches at Gile, 38 inches in Hurley, 22.5 near Mellen, 20 near Iron River, 18 near Butternut, 17 in Ashland, 17 near Clam Lake, 16 in Sarona, 16 near Stone Lake, 15 in Grantsburg, 15 near Spooner, 14 near Seeley, 13 near Hayward, 12.5 near Bayfield, 12 near Phillips, 12 in Poplar and Foxboro, 12 on Madeline Island, and about 9.5 inches near Superior.

An early winter storm brought nearly a foot of snow to portions of west central Wisconsin, Monday, November 10th. ||This storm began to develop across the Dakotas late Sunday evening, and quickly moved east and northeast across southern Minnesota and into west central Wisconsin by early Monday morning, November 10th. The surface weather map had a developing area of low pressure across Nebraska Sunday evening. This low pressure center moved east-northeast across Iowa, and into the Great Lakes region by Monday night. ||Light snow began across west central Wisconsin after 3 am, Monday morning. It quickly moved north and east before stalling across northern St. Croix, Barron and Rusk counties creating an area of heavy snowfall accumulations. ||There was even a period of sleet and freezing rain on the southern edge of the snowfall which created very icy conditions on area roadways. The mixed precipitation was mainly south of a line from New Richmond to Ladysmith, Wisconsin. ||The worst conditions were between 6 am and 3 pm in west central Wisconsin. The heaviest snowfall was north of Highway 8. One of the heaviest snowfall totals in Wisconsin was 11.0 inches at Rice Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers, local officials and the general public measured or estimated between 8 to 10 inches on the far northern side of the county, to around 4 inches in the far southern part. In addition, sleet and freezing rain mixed with the snow during the morning and early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow as a low pressure system intensified northeast of Wisconsin.  Five to seven inches of snow fell in 12 hours across parts of northeast Wisconsin.  Winds gusted to more than 30 mph at times causing blowing and drifting of the new snow.  The highest snowfall totals included 7.6 inches at Stevens Point (Portage Co.), 7.4 inches at Polonia (Portage Co.), 7.3 inches near Amherst (Portage Co.), 7.2 inches near Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 7.1 inches at Lena (Oconto Co.), and 7.0 inches at High Falls Reservoir (southern Marinette Co.) and Oconto Falls (southern Oconto Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the northwest Waushara County.

Heavy rainfall of 3-5 inches from April 12-14 resulted in minor river flooding of the Milwaukee and Wisconsin rivers. The flooding closed roads and did flood some homes near the Wisconsin river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wisconsin river crested at 17.84 feet in Portage. Long Lake which is near the Wisconsin river also flooded. Several roads were closed in the Black Hawk Park area with up to 4 feet of water over the roads. Several homes and out buildings had water in the first floor in the town of Dekorra. Some people had to use boats for transportation to and from their home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused the Wisconsin River at Portage to rise above flood stage at 17.08 feet for a brief period. This caused flooding of the lower roads in the Blackhawk Park area, which is between the Wisconsin River and Long Lake. The roads were closed with a few feet of water and ice on the roads. The water and ice is very near to several small homes with a small number of people having to canoe or walk to dry land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused the Wisconsin River at Portage to rise above flood stage at 17.08 feet for a brief period. Minor lowland flooding was already occurring from late November and continued well into December. This caused flooding of the lower roads in the Blackhawk Park area, which is between the Wisconsin River and Long Lake. The roads were closed with a few feet of water and ice on the roads. The water and ice is very near to several small homes with a small number of people having to canoe or walk to dry land.

A complex storm system affected the region November 10-11, 2014, bringing a swath of heavy snow to parts of northern Wisconsin.  The main storm hit in two phases.  The first phase was in the form of a long west to east band of snow which set up across Minnesota and northern Wisconsin early on November 10th.  Low pressure then moved across far southern Wisconsin and produced another round of snow late on November 10th and into the 11th across central and northern Wisconsin.||In addition to the long-duration storm, additional lake effect snow from Lake Superior pushed totals over 20 inches in some locations in the snowbelt.  The highest reported snowfall total was 23.7 inches at Lac du Flambeau (Vilas Co.).  Some of the highest snow totals outside the snowbelt included 15.2 inches near Alvin (Forest Co.), 12.5 inches at Florence (Florence Co.), 12.0 inches at Lake Tomahawk (Oneida Co.), and 11 inches at Pearson (Langlade Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 23.7 inches of snow was measured at Lac du Flambeau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm air aloft and an upper level front brought several inches of powdery snow to portions of south central Wisconsin, including Madison and locations to the  south and west of Madison. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during the morning rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of powdery snow fell overnight and into the morning commute. The heaviest totals were west and south of Madison. There were numerous slide-offs and accidents which led to significant traffics delays on the Beltline, and on portions of the Interstates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold wave affected southern Wisconsin during this period. West to northwest winds of 10-20 mph with the passage of an arctic cold front brought wind chill temperatures of 20 below to 38 below zero Fahrenheit beginning the early morning of January 27th, and not ending until the morning of January 29th. The coldest period was the morning of January 28th when wind chills ranged from 30 below to 38 below zero. Widespread school and business closings occurred during this time. The Governor declared a state of emergency due to a propane shortage across the state. Numerous water main breaks and frozen laterals continued to occur throughout the entire month of January. Two cold weather deaths occurred, one in Rock County and one in Milwaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 20 below to 30 below zero. One cold weather death was reported by the Rock County Coroner. A man collapsed outside due to a heart attack and the extreme cold.

The Governor declared a state of emergency due to a propane shortage across the state. Numerous water main breaks and frozen laterals continued to occur throughout the entire month of January. Two cold weather deaths occurred, one in Rock County and one in Milwaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill temperatures hovered around 35 below zero from the late evening into the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front had stalled over southern Wisconsin with repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. There was some flash flooding in southeast Wisconsin.
Flooding along the Trempealeau River near Dodge (Trempealeau County) occurred in early April due to run off from snow melt and ice on the river breaking up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with the ice breaking up, caused the Trempealeau River in Dodge to go out of its banks. The river crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 9.33 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southwest Wisconsin. A round of storms developed during the early morning hours of June 19th along a warm front and then a second round developed during the afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front. Radar estimates of 3 to 6 inches of rainfall were common across southern Crawford into Grant Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused a mud and rock slide to occur in Potosi closing State Highway 133.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across western Wisconsin on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County). All the schools across western Wisconsin were closed on both January 27th and 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Trempealeau County from the early morning of January 27th into the morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front had stalled over southern Wisconsin with repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. There was some flash flooding in southeast Wisconsin.

Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across western Wisconsin on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County). All the schools across western Wisconsin were closed on both January 27th and 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Adams County from the early morning of January 27th into the morning of the 28th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 40 below from a mesonet reporting station in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 30th across southwest Wisconsin ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across the area, they primarily produced strong winds that blew down trees and power lines. A measured gust of 78 mph occurred near Lancaster (Grant County) with other reports of 60 to 70 mph gusts across Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Patch Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of June 1st, thunderstorms with very heavy rain rolled across western Wisconsin. Some of these storms dumped over 3 inches of rain which caused streams in the vicinity of Westby (Vernon County) to rapidly come out of their banks spilling water over some roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of low lying agricultural land occurred southwest of Westby after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of July 31st over northern Wisconsin along a trough of low pressure. As these storms moved southeast, they produced some hail over portions of north central Wisconsin. Penny sized hail was reported near Gilman (Taylor County) and Thorp (Clark County).

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of July 31st over northern Wisconsin along a trough of low pressure. As these storms moved southeast, they produced some hail over portions of north central Wisconsin. Penny sized hail was reported near Gilman (Taylor County) and Thorp (Clark County).

Thunderstorms developed across western Wisconsin during the evening hours of June 28th ahead of a trough of low pressure. Several of these storms produced high winds that caused considerable damage in the city of La Crosse north into Holmen (La Crosse County). Numerous trees and power lines were blown down by winds that gusted to at least 60 mph. At least 7,000 customers in the La Crosse area were without power. The power outage took out street lights and stop lights across much of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near the intersection of Green Bay Street and East Ave in the city of La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front. A bow echo produced marginal wind damage in Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe straight line winds knocked down several trees onto County Road K. Other isolated reports of branches and trees down were received from south of Oxford east to near Packwaukee, but the wind speeds were likely below 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms raced across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours producing straight line wind damage and one brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado was observed ripping apart trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small segment Bow Echo ahead of a cold front accelerated east across Kenosha County. A mesovortex developed along the leading edge of the bow echo and produced significant straight line wind damage on the south side of Kenosha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3.6 mile west to east path of significant straight line wind damage. The most concentrated damage was just south of highway 50 and east of Green Bay Road. Numerous large trees snapped and uprooted. Shingle damage to approximately 8 homes with two roofs likely requiring replacement. Many privacy fences destroyed. A large tree fell on a home. The Burger King sign was destroyed. A camper was overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall contributed to a large tree falling and landing on a tent at Devils Lake State Park, killing a 10 year old girl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree uprooted and landed on a tent killing a 10 year old girl and injuring her father and brother.  The tree fell approximately 1 hour after thunderstorms with heavy rains. Nearby observations and Doppler radar suggest around 1.90 inches of rain fell. The roots of the tree grew over a large rock and the soil was saturated from the heavy rain. These factors may have weakened the stability of the tree and it fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms move east across southern Wisconsin during the morning and produced wind damage and heavy rainfall in Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated one inch of rain in 20 minutes resulted in urban street flooding with some street closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northwest Wisconsin, on Monday (July 21st). A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin. Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex. This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and then northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd). This derecho brought winds of at least 50 to 70 mph to parts of northwest Wisconsin, which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5 to 6 inch diameter tree was broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided in the first week of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late June on the Mississippi River in La Crosse ended in the first week of July when the river went below the flood stage. The river crested 1.5 feet above the flood stage at 13.48 feet. The high water caused officials to close two public beaches in the city of La Crosse, a campground and to cancel several water events during the city's Riverfest festival.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved northward across west central Wisconsin during the afternoon of Friday, June 27th. One of the storms produced a tornado around Colfax that had a damage track of 7 miles. The worst damage was at the local high school where damage to the school HVAC units on the roof occurred, plus several storage sheds and an athletic metal building were completely destroyed.  Lightning also struck during the Cadott Country Fest, with minor injuries to two people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey indicated that most of the damage was rated EF-0 with winds up to 85 mph. However, the tornado did briefly strengthen to EF-1 on the northeast side of Colfax, at the local high school where a shed was destroyed and two HVAC units where blown off the roof. This is where the winds may have reached 90 mph. The tornado was often less than 25 yards wide but it did widen to a maximum of 100 yards at the local high school athletic field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm brought large hail to northwest Wisconsin on June 17th.

A few miles west of downtown Rhinelander, the automated weather observation system at Rhinelander Airport recorded 1.55 inches of rain in 19 minutes, including 0.81 inch in just five minutes between 7:02 PM and 7:07 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed along a cold front produced isolated wind damage and large hail.  The storms downed trees, tree limbs, and power lines in central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted at least a dozen trees in saturated soil on the southeast side of Wisconsin Rapids. Two houses were damaged by the fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure that tracked from the Central Plains into Lower Michigan brought moderate to heavy snow and windy conditions, resulting in blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibilities. Far southeast and east central Wisconsin experienced the most hazardous winter conditions. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially within the Milwaukee Metropolitan area. Some schools and businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4-6 inches with southeast wind gusts of 30-35 mph. Heavy snow and blowing snow with visibilities less than 1/4 mile was reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability interacted with an upper level storm system.  Scattered storms developed and produced hail up to quarter size.  The slow movement of the storms resulted in locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell south of Port Edwards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the morning hours of September 4th. These storms produced high winds and large hail as they moved across portions of north central Wisconsin. All of the reports came from Taylor County where a 60 mph wind gust and quarter sized hail occurred near Jump River while trees were blown down in Gilman and west of Medford. Additional thunderstorms developed during the afternoon as a cold front moved across the region. These storms primarily produced locally heavy rain that caused some street flooding to occur in and south of Abbottsford (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Jump River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of copious moisture, an approaching upper level system, and a warm front across Wisconsin resulted in the formation of thunderstorms along the front.  The storms produced heavy rain, causing isolated flash flooding, as they trained across the same area.  The storms also dropped large hail and a brief tornado developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed and caused minor damage as it moved east near Shady Lane in rural northern Winnebago County. The weak tornado (EF0) caused two single wood power poles to partially lean in saturated soil (DI 24, DOD 1) and knocked over a few trees and limbs (DI 28, DOD 2 and 3). Swirl marks could be seen in a field of tall grass. The tornado was not in contact with the ground during the entire path length. Peak winds were estimated at 65 to 75 mph.

The farm was located on highway C, northeast of Union Grove and southwest of the Ives Grove area of Yorkville. Also, large tree branches fell in two separate areas of the city of Racine landing in the streets. One large tree branch landed on a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northwest Wisconsin, on Monday (July 21st). A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin. Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex. This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and then northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd). This derecho brought winds of at least 50 to 70 mph to parts of northwest Wisconsin, which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree about 24 inches in diameter was blown down onto a fence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm that quickly developed west of Abrams killed two men working on a tree house at a home on County Road E in the Town of Morgan.  The men were struck by lightning from the storm as it passed about two miles to their south.  One of the men, a 27-year-old, was killed instantly.  The other, a 31-year-old, was taken to a Green Bay hospital and died of his injuries on August 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hayward area experienced a very wet period in late August and early September which brought about 4 to 7 inches of rain to the area. This included a particular large and heavy rain producing thunderstorm that went right through Hayward early on September 4th. The rains from that thunderstorm did not cause substantial flooding. However, all the rain over the past days caused Smith Lake, which is north of Hayward, to flood. The rising waters of Smith Lake gradually overtopped the earthen dam that feeds into Smith Creek, which in turn runs through Hayward proper and feeds into Hayward Lake. The flooding along Smith Creek late September 5th affected at least several roads and many homes in the Shue's Pond area into September 7th. The city had to sandbag areas of the city. County and city officials requested the National Weather Service issue a flood warning to help get the word out.
Thunderstorms brought large hail to parts of Northwest Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening on September 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft and a passing upper level system brought scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms to northeast Wisconsin.  There was an extended period of hail, some large, that wiped out millions of dollars worth of crops north of Sturgeon Bay.  The hail accumulated to a depth of 3 to 5 inches and snowplows were needed to clear it from a couple of roads in the area.  The University of Wisconsin-Sturgeon Bay Research Farm suffered losses due to the hail and recorded 3.05 inches of precipitation from the storm.  Despite temperatures well above freezing overnight, hail was still on the ground at daybreak on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail caused significant crop damage in a nearly 1.5 mile wide and 3.2 mile long swath southwest and south of Institute. In addition to the large hail, copious amounts of smaller hail accumulated to a depth of three to five inches in some locations. ||Apple, cherry and corn crops in the area each sustained at least a million dollars worth of damage. In addition, damage was also reported to grape and winter wheat crops.  Some apple trees were damaged so severely that they need to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, in unstable air ahead of an upper level disturbance, produced hail and wind damage as they passed through Wood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged trees and flipped a storage shed upside down, landing it next to another shed.  The damage occurred near Arpin, at Highway 186 and CTH-K.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms formed over the southern end of Chequamegon Bay just to the west Ashland, WI on during the early afternoon of August 11th.  At least two witnesses saw a waterspout form over the Bay and then briefly move inland as a tornado with the storms moving to the south-southwest.  Pictures were taken of the waterspout.  One witness described the tornado moved inland for about 10 seconds before dissipating near the median on Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least two witnesses saw a waterspout form over Chequamegon Bay and then briefly move inland as a tornado with the storms moving to the south-southwest. Pictures were taken of the waterspout. One witness described the tornado moved inland for about 10 seconds before dissipating near the median on Highway 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across Wood County were mostly around 6 inches.  The highest total was 6.3 inches at Wisconsin Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving weather system dropped 5 to 7 inches of snow in 12 hours across central and east central Wisconsin as it moved across the state.  The highest snowfall totals included 7.8 inches in Chilton (Calumet Co.), 7.5 inches in Ephraim (Door Co.), and 7.2 inches in Cleveland (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 6 inches of snow fell in 12 hours across parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable air mass ahead of an approaching cold front.  Clusters of storms with torrential rain caused flooding and flash flooding.  Rainfall totals were mainly in the 1 to 3 inch range, with locally higher amounts.  The heaviest rainfall was across northern Door County where more than 6 inches of rain fell in the Baileys Harbor area, flooding basements and campgrounds and washing out the shoulders of roads.  The flooding caused damage to some documents at the Ephraim Historical Society.  Isolated storms produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a building on the east side of Green Bay, damaging its roof and facade.  The time of this event is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a wet microburst that uprooted or snapped about a dozen trees near Little Sturgeon Bay. Winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system moved across northern Wisconsin and produced locally heavy snow across Vilas County.  The snow was enhanced by lake effect as cold air poured across Lake Superior.  Twelve-hour snowfall totals were in the 6 to 9 inch range.||North winds were 20 to 30 mph across the region, with gusts in excess of 50 mph in Door County.   Several trees were downed by the strong winds across northeast Wisconsin, causing sporadic power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 56 mph was measured at Northport Pier at Death's Door.  Sustained winds there were in the 35 to 45 mph range from 1:40 AM CST to 3:30 AM CST.  A gust to 50 mph was measured on Washington Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across east central Wisconsin, producing several measured gusts to around 50 mph, as low pressure passed east of the state.  The winds associated with the low may have been enhanced by a gravity wave.  The strong winds downed numerous trees, branches, and power lines.  More than 14,000 customers were without power at the height of the event.||Some of the strongest measured wind gusts included: 53 mph at Austin Straubel International Airport (Brown Co.), 52 mph at Shawano Municipal Airport (Shawano Co.), and 51 mph at Outagamie County Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 52 mph was measured at Shawano Municipal Airport.

More than 14,000 customers were without power at the height of the event.||Some of the strongest measured wind gusts included: 53 mph at Austin Straubel International Airport (Brown Co.), 52 mph at Shawano Municipal Airport (Shawano Co.), and 51 mph at Outagamie County Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed numerous trees (especially in about a 10 mile stretch north of Iola), branches, and power lines across Waupaca County.  A wind gust of 48 mph was measured at Clintonville Municipal Airport, and a gust to 40 mph was measured at Waupaca Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable air mass ahead of an approaching cold front.  Clusters of storms with torrential rain caused flooding and flash flooding.  Rainfall totals were mainly in the 1 to 3 inch range, with locally higher amounts. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded basements, resorts, and campgrounds, and damaged roads in the Sister Bay, Fish Creek, and Baileys Harbor areas.  In excess of 6 inches of rain in Baileys Harbor caused nearly every home there to sustain a backup from the sewage disposal plant.  Flood waters damaged documents at the Ephraim Historical Society.  The flooding also washed out shoulders of several roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed north of a warm front and propagated east across northern Wisconsin.  The storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, large hail, and heavy rainfall.  The most significant wind damage occurred near Townsend (Oconto Co.) where estimated gusts to 75 mph downed at least 30 trees, causing roof damage to a dozen homes.  Golf ball to tennis ball size hail fell in Oneida County near Rhinelander and Lake Tomahawk.  Rainfall totals from the storms ranged from 3 to 3.5 inches at Lake Tomahawk (Oneida Co.), Three Lakes (Oneida Co.), and Lac du Flambeau (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Hortonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was killed when his truck slid into the path of a freight train while snow was falling across western Wisconsin. According to official reports, heavy, wet snow was falling as the driver approached a rail crossing north of Fountain City (Buffalo County). He was unable to stop when the crossing arm closed and slid into the path of the train.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driver of a vehicle was killed when he slid into the path of an oncoming freight train north of Fountain City. The accident occurred on slippery roads created by the heavy, wet snow that was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed north of a warm front and propagated east across northern Wisconsin.  The storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, large hail, and heavy rainfall.  The most significant wind damage occurred near Townsend (Oconto Co.) where estimated gusts to 75 mph downed at least 30 trees, causing roof damage to a dozen homes.  Golf ball to tennis ball size hail fell in Oneida County near Rhinelander and Lake Tomahawk.  Rainfall totals from the storms ranged from 3 to 3.5 inches at Lake Tomahawk (Oneida Co.), Three Lakes (Oneida Co.), and Lac du Flambeau (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced hail up to tennis ball size southeast of Lake Tomahawk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of north central Wisconsin received up to ten inches of snow from the first winter storm of the season. Two rounds of snow impacted the region as an area of low pressure moved from Nebraska into lower Michigan from November 10th into the 11th. The first round of snow moved over the region during the morning of the 10th with the second round falling during the late afternoon of the 10th into the early afternoon of the 11th. Between these two rounds of snow, Taylor County was hit the hardest with accumulations around 6 inches in the southwest part of the county up to 10 inches in the northeast corner. Emergency management officials reported several vehicle accidents occurred because of the snowy conditions with only one serious injury. The driver of a vehicle that was attempting to pull out a stuck vehicle, sustained a head injury when the ball hitch on the stuck vehicle broke loose, flew threw the back window of other vehicle, striking the driver in the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across Taylor County. One person was seriously injured when the ball hitch of a stuck vehicle broke loose, flew threw the window of another vehicle and struck the driver of the second vehicle in the head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of relatively mild and moist air aloft flowing over below freezing temperatures at the surface resulted in light freezing rain from late evening on November 21st to just beyond sunrise on November 22nd. A light coating of ice formed on untreated roads and sidewalks with vehicle slide-offs, accidents, and falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of ice accumulation on roads and sidewalks from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system trekked from southeast Kansas to northeast Wisconsin on February 20th. As the storm system deepened, it produced a shield of heavy snow and embedded thunderstorms on its northwestern flank, which produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. A rain/snow mix began across much of the area during the morning of the 20th as temperatures hovered in the middle 30s, but precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy snow by early afternoon. The snow continued through the early morning hours of the 21st before tapering off. ||As the low tracked through Wisconsin and into the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, northwest winds increased to between 20 and 30 mph, with gusts to between 40 and 50 mph.||Authorities reported a partial roof collapse and gas leak at the Kmart in Eau Claire. Officials believe the weight of snow on the flat roof was the cause of the collapse.||During the height of the storm, Xcel Energy reported that a total of 13,000 customers in northern and western Wisconsin experienced power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several resources from trained spotters, local public, and county officials measured over 6 inches of snowfall in a 12 hour period. Locally, 8 inches fell near Stockholm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought, that had persisted across the northern portions of Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties into the northwest corner of Jackson County, ended in late February. Enough moisture from the snow that fell over the winter was able to work into the ground and began recharging the soil moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought across northern Trempealeau County ended in late February. Enough moisture from the snow that fell over the winter was able to work into the ground and began recharging the soil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm hit portions of north central Wisconsin with ice. The coating of ice, up to a quarter of an inch thick, mainly coated elevated exposed surfaces. The icing did create some power outages in Thorp (Clark County). After the freezing rain ended, a period of snow occurred with accumulations of 1 to 2 inches. The highest reported snow total was 1.7 inches near Owen (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain coated elevated, exposed surfaces with up to a quarter inch of ice causing power outages in Thorp. COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 1 to 2 inches of snow across Clark County. The highest reported total was 1.7 inches near Owen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep low pressure system moved from the southern Rockies through Iowa and central Wisconsin, bringing widespread heavy snow to northwest Wisconsin. There was widespread snowfall of 8 to 15 across northwest Wisconsin, and some areas received even more.  Specific snowfall reports included 21 in Gile, 18 in Superior, 16.5 in Poplar, 16 in Seeley, 16 near Washburn, 13 in Ashland, 10 in Hertel, 9 near Springbrook, and 8.8 near Park Falls. ||The precipitation began as light rain for parts of northwest Wisconsin where temperatures warmed to near 40 degrees, but it was short-lived and quickly changed to snow during the late afternoon and evening of the 20th.  Strong winds developed in the wake of the storm, with gusts of about 30 to 40 mph. This caused major blowing and drifting snow, and low visibility.
The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported the death of an elderly man from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Examiner's office reported the death of an elderly man from hypothermia. The man fell outside his home suffering a cervical fracture, and then died from hypothermia. The temperatures were in the 30s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong stationary front with a 40 degree temperature gradient setup along the Wisconsin and Illinois border. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed and moved east to northeast across southern Wisconsin. Dozens of hail reports were received with only a couple wind damage reports. Heavy rain lead to flash flooding of small creeks in southern Sauk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed over southern Sauk County due to flash flooding of small creeks and streams. Estimated rainfall amounts ranged from 4-7 inches. Some of the known creeks that flooded were Otter Creek, Honey Creek, North Branch of Honey Creek, and Pine Creek. There was one bridge washout at the north end of Stones Pocket Road due to the flooding of Otter Creek. This will cost approximately $150,000 to replace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported a death of a homeless woman due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's office reported a death of a homeless woman due to hypothermia. She had been living in her vehicle. Temperatures earlier in the morning were in the 20s with temperatures in the lower 30s when she was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening low pressure area moved north through Wisconsin with strong west to northwest winds in its wake. The strong winds began with the passage of the occluded front during the evening of February 20th. The winds weakened by late afternoon or evening on February 21st. The high winds contributed to the cause of a multi-vehicle accident that involved one death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds out of the northwest were a factor in a multi-vehicle accident that killed a 86 year old man near the intersection of Highway 11 and Dunbar Road in the Town of Plymouth. The car driven by the 86 year old man crossed the center-line going westbound and collided with an eastbound Freightliner. Wind gusts were as high as 46 mph at the Janesville airport and the strong winds may have pushed the car across the center-line.
