A Polar trough brought a narrow north to south band of light snow that slowly moved east across southeast Wisconsin. The air and road temperatures, which were above freezing during the day and early evening, cooled below freezing with icy roads quickly developing. Dozens of slide-offs and accidents occurred including a 30-vehicle pileup on highway 45 in Wauwatosa. This was the last winter weather event that occurred in spring. Due to the very cold and snowy cold season, the Wisconsin Department of Transportation is requesting $27.3 million from the state legislature to cover the counties costs of additional salt and plowing. The $27.3 million is the total for the entire state of Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Air and road temperatures cooled below freezing during the evening as light snow was occurring. Wet roads quickly become icy and dozens of slide-offs and accidents occurred. Portions of I-43 and I-94 were shut down due to the accidents. No serious injuries were reported.

Due to the very cold and snowy cold season, the Wisconsin Department of Transportation is requesting $27.3 million from the state legislature to cover the counties costs of additional salt and plowing. The $27.3 million is the total for the entire state of Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Air and road temperatures cooled below freezing during the evening as light snow was occurring. Wet roads quickly become icy and dozens of slide-offs and accidents occurred. A 30 car pileup, which included two trucks and two buses, occurred on highway 45 in Wauwatosa. Only one injury was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along portions of the Kickapoo River in southwest Wisconsin in late March. The flooding was a result of 0.75 to 1.25 inches of rain that fell combined with runoff from snow melt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and snow melt pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Viola. The river crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 14.33 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system trekked from southeast Kansas to northeast Wisconsin on February 20th. As the storm system deepened, it produced a shield of heavy snow and embedded thunderstorms on its northwestern flank, which produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. A rain/snow mix began across much of the area during the morning of the 20th as temperatures hovered in the middle 30s, but precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy snow by early afternoon. The snow continued through the early morning hours of the 21st before tapering off. ||As the low tracked through Wisconsin and into the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, northwest winds increased to between 20 and 30 mph, with gusts to between 40 and 50 mph.||Authorities reported a partial roof collapse and gas leak at the Kmart in Eau Claire. Officials believe the weight of snow on the flat roof was the cause of the collapse.||During the height of the storm, Xcel Energy reported that a total of 13,000 customers in northern and western Wisconsin experienced power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several resources from trained spotters, local public, and county officials measured over 6 inches of snowfall in a 12 hour period. Locally, 8 inches fell near Eau Claire. A local store had a partial roof collapse due to the heavy snowfall in Eau Claire.  In Altoona, firefighters rescued a man pinned underneath a collapsed awning on his mobile home due to the snow. He was taken to the hospital for treatment of a leg injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed across the Central Plains Thursday morning, April 3rd. Initially, warm air brought areas of light rain, sleet, and snow across portions of east central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin during the morning and early afternoon. This area of precipitation quickly moved northward into Lake Superior, and another area of precipitation developed across northwest Iowa, spread northeast across southwest and south central Minnesota during the afternoon hours, then encompassed most of west central Wisconsin by the evening. The precipitation quickly became more sleet and snow as colder air moved into the region.  Eventually all of the precipitation moved east of the area by the afternoon of Friday, April 4th. ||Some of the higher snowfall totals from Wisconsin include;|10.0 inches in Haugen, in Barron County. |9.0 inches in Baldwin, in St. Croix County. |9.0 inches in Cumberland, in Barron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations, and trained spotters reported 6 to 10 inches of snowfall across Barron County, with a local report of 10.0 inches near Haugen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa. As the storms rolled across western Wisconsin they produced sporadic wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes in Platteville (Grant County) where 5 injuries occurred. The tornadoes caused extensive damage to the University of Wisconsin - Platteville campus and to numerous homes and business near the campus. Damage estimates to the campus alone were near $10 million. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for three southern Wisconsin counties, including Grant. The U.S. Small Business Administration approved a request for a disaster declaration in Grant County. The disaster declaration allows homeowners and businesses to obtain loans to repair or replace damaged or destroyed property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed along a squall line west of Platteville, just south of County Road B, and traveled through Memorial Park on to the Univ. of Wisconsin - Platteville campus. Five people were injured, one of them seriously. There was extensive damage done to five buildings and the stadium on campus.  Roofs of two new residential halls were partially destroyed and a third residential hall was also damaged along with one academic building. Two large lighting structures at the football stadium were knocked over, with one crashing across the bleachers causing the concrete foundation underneath the bleachers to be substantially moved. Damage continued into nearby homes and businesses as the tornado tracked across Business Highway 151. The top level of a split level home was ripped off one house, another house lost part of a roof and a porch while several other homes had windows blown out and trees knocked down. A gas station/convenience store was leveled by the tornado where it also ripped a pump out of the ground and blew over a semi. Numerous other business and vehicles suffered blown out windows. After hitting a cemetery on the south side of town, the tornado dissipated just before reaching U.S. Highway 151 southeast of town. A total of 12 homes were destroyed with another 20 damaged.||At UW-Platteville, the tornado caused $7.3 million in damage to Bridgeway Commons, $4.8 million in damage to Rountree Commons and $1.8 million to Southwest Hall. All three buildings are dorms.||The tornado caused $1.8 million in damage to Engineering Hall, a classroom building, and $1.7 million in damage to the stadium.

Some of these storms dumped over 3 inches of rain which caused streams in the vicinity of Westby (Vernon County) to rapidly come out of their banks spilling water over some roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Viroqua, 3.7 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary intense band of snow set up from southwest through northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin as low pressure tracked just to the south.  Temperatures held steady in the upper 20s to lower 30s within the band of snow, however just to the south they reached the 40s and 50s.  A sharp rain/snow line also remained nearly stationary, which led to extreme snowfall gradients across the area.  ||A swath of 8 to 12 inches stretched from just west of Hutchinson, MN northeast to northwestern Wisconsin.  There may have been higher snow totals in northwestern Polk County, but no confirmed reports were received from that area. ||The following are some of the higher storm totals from this storm:|12.0 inches in St. Croix Falls. |9.0 inches north of Bruce. |6.9 inches at the Rice Lake Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from local observers, and county officials indicated a large area of snowfall across the county. Amounts ranged from near 4 to 6 inches across the southern part of the county, to 6 to 8 inches in the northern part.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor mudslides occurred across the extreme southern part of Crawford County north of Boscobel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pulse thunderstorm produced a wet microburst and resultant tree damage in Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst uprooted one large Oak tree and snapped another large tree in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that weakened across southwest Wisconsin during the early evening of Monday, June 16th, developed an area of damaging outflow winds. The worst area was in Rusk County where several large trees were blown down due to these strong outflow winds. Other reports of wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph were reported from various AWOS sites in Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted near Island Lake. Damage occurred with a larger branch that fell on a garage roof, and a cargo trailer near the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of June 1st, thunderstorms with very heavy rain rolled across western Wisconsin. Some of these storms dumped over 3 inches of rain which caused streams in the vicinity of Westby (Vernon County) to rapidly come out of their banks spilling water over some roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer east of Westby measured 3.46 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front had stalled over southern Wisconsin with repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. There was some flash flooding in southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail diameter ranged from 1 to 1.75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms developed in a warm, humid air mass ahead of a cold front.  The storms produced as much as 4.75 inches of rain near Porterfield (Marinette Co.).  Farm fields were flooded and low-lying roads were covered by water.  Heavy rainfall also caused flooding of low-lying areas west of Marathon City (Marathon Co.).||In addition to the heavy rainfall, the storms produced small hail, and winds of 50 mph that uprooted a few trees in the wet soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced small hail and two periods of heavy rainfall with up to 4.75 inches of rain in the Porterfield area.  Runoff from the heavy rain caused lowland flooding in the Townships of Porterfield, Lake and Grover.  Wind gusts to 50 mph uprooted some trees in the saturated soil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed well to the north of a warm front that pushed northeast from northern Illinois into southwest Wisconsin.  The storms passed through central and north central Wisconsin, producing isolated penny to nickel size hail in Wood County and Portage County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced nickel size hail at the intersection of Michigan Avenue and Patch Street in Stevens Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed well to the north of a warm front that pushed northeast from northern Illinois into southwest Wisconsin.  The storms passed through central and north central Wisconsin, producing isolated penny to nickel size hail in Wood County and Portage County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced penny size hail in Wisconsin Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms produced a couple wet microbursts and one large hail event. A heavy rainfall event occurred near Ripon, WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 foot by 120 foot pole barn was mostly leveled. The steel sheeting from the barn was blown about 1/4 mile to the northeast. The collapse of the pole barn damaged tractors, a combine, and other farm equipment. Another farm lost roofs from two sheds and several large trees were downed with a fallen tree totaling a car. At yet another residence, fallen trees damaged two buildings. Finally another residence had a 16 inch spruce tree snapped off at the base and it partially landed on the home damaging the sofitts and gutters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms produced a couple wet microbursts and one large hail event. A heavy rainfall event occurred near Ripon, WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street and parking low flooding led to water rushing into a business on highway 23 just east of Ripon. One hour rainfall estimated by doppler radar was 1.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms occurred Thursday afternoon and evening as warm air surged northward across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest. A complex of severe thunderstorms that developed near Waseca, Minnesota, moved northeast across the southern Metro area, and into west central Wisconsin. Damaging wind and large hail occurred with this bow type thunderstorm complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and the associated surface trough triggered clusters of showers and thunderstorms over southern Wisconsin during the evening. A thunderstorm became rooted to a pseudo lake breeze/outflow boundary moving inland from Lake Michigan. The thunderstorm developed supercell characteristics and dropped a downburst on the west side of Waukesha. The thunderstorm them morphed into a small segment squall line   with damaging winds on the leading gust front as it moved through Kenosha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A southward moving west to east oriented gust front downed several trees just east of Silver Lake and southward to Camp Lake. One tree landed on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low and associated cold front triggered thunderstorms across southern Wisconsin from the late morning through the afternoon. A pulse thunderstorm produced large hail in Sheboygan County.

One low-topped supercell thunderstorm formed as an upper level low pressure area moved across southern Wisconsin. The supercell produced large hail over several counties and a brief tornado in Green County.

One low-topped supercell thunderstorm formed as an upper level low pressure area moved across southern Wisconsin. The supercell produced large hail over several counties and a brief tornado in Green County.

Gusty west to southwest winds brought a hot and very humid airmass into southern Wisconsin just ahead of an approaching cold front. High temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s and dewpoints in the middle to upper 70s resulted in heat index values in the lower 100s for portions of southern Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat Index values in the lower 100s.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets impassable in downtown Madison and the near east side of the city due to deep water with multiple vehicles being swept away or pushed into oncoming traffic. The northbound side of South Park street intersecting with Wingra Drive was flooded. Some buildings were flooded near the Capital building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed north of a stationary front, that was over southern Wisconsin, and produced heavy rainfall, isolated large hail and wind damage. Some of the highest measured 24-hour rainfall totals, ending around 0700 CST, included 3.80 inches at Babcock (Wood Co.), 3.45 inches near Vesper (Wood Co.), 2.46 inches at Pittsville (Wood Co.), 2.41 inches northeast of Wild Rose (Waushara Co.), 2.32 inches at Almond (Portage Co.), and 2.23 inches at Plover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced penny size hail near Gleason.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 7th. These storms became severe as they moved from central to east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. There was a brief tornado touchdown in southern Washington County at the St. Croix Bluffs Regional Park, and it moved eastward across the St. Croix River and caused minor damage to trees before lifting about a mile into Pierce County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado continued to move eastward from Washington County, into Pierce County, Wisconsin, where most of the damage was large tree limbs blown down from mature hardwood trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across western Wisconsin during the evening hours of June 28th ahead of a trough of low pressure. Several of these storms produced high winds that caused considerable damage in the city of La Crosse north into Holmen (La Crosse County). Numerous trees and power lines were blown down by winds that gusted to at least 60 mph. At least 7,000 customers in the La Crosse area were without power. The power outage took out street lights and stop lights across much of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches were blown down and moved a distance of at least 100 feet. A electrical transformer was also blown out on the northeast side of Onalaska.

A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa. As the storms rolled across western Wisconsin they produced sporadic wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes in Platteville (Grant County) where 5 injuries occurred. The tornadoes caused extensive damage to the University of Wisconsin - Platteville campus and to numerous homes and business near the campus. Damage estimates to the campus alone were near $10 million. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for three southern Wisconsin counties, including Grant. The U.S. Small Business Administration approved a request for a disaster declaration in Grant County. The disaster declaration allows homeowners and businesses to obtain loans to repair or replace damaged or destroyed property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Hillsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing cold front over northeast Iowa during the evening of June 29th. As these storms moved across southwest Wisconsin they produced a tornado near Fennimore (Grant County) that produced considerable tree damage, destroyed several farm outbuildings and damaged three homes. The storms also produced damaging winds that blew over vehicles near Mt. Hope (Grant County) and Fennimore. Barns were blown down near Sinsinawa (Grant County) and Louisburg (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was blown over east of Mt. Hope along U.S. Highway 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Quasi-linear convective system with a cyclonic rotating comma head on the north end produced numerous tornadoes and damaging straight line winds. Governor Walker declared a State of Emergency for Green and Dane counties due to the extensive damage from severe thunderstorms and tornadoes that occurred from June 16-18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado snapped and uprooted numerous large trees with some trees landing on homes and cars. Power poles and power lines knocked down. A roof removed from one home and a roof removed from a business. Shingle damage to other homes. Boats and small sailboats sunk or missing. An 80 pound canoe and two kayaks thrown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Tornado severely damaged the Country View Elementary school, which will have to be rebuilt. At least 30 homes sustained major damage, 19 of which were rendered uninhabitable. Many trees down. Estimated winds up to 140 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado damaged at least 23 homes with some homes losing much of their roofs. Widespread uprooted and snapped trees within this heavily wooded neighborhood. Some large trees landing on homes.  A few roofs had been pierced by 2-by-4s. Estimated winds up to 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak front in a very moist atmosphere and produced heavy rain in Calumet County and Winnebago County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in parts of central and southern Calumet County. 3.38 of rain fell near Stockbridge in less than three hours. The heavy rain caused fields to flood and washed out culverts of several area roads. Several houses had water in their basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of June 23rd across western Wisconsin. Some of these storms produced funnel clouds over the northern sections of the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and inflow from tributary streams pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in La Crosse. The flooding continued into July with the crest occurring in July as well. The flooding closed 130 of the 400 campsites at Goose Island County Park and all 300 of the campsites at Pettibone Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak and brief waterspout formed under a rain shower over southern Lake Winnebago. The shower developed as a result of an upper level low pressure system that was over Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak and brief waterspout formed under a rain shower over southern Lake Winnebago, about 5 miles southeast of Oshkosh. The waterspout, which was photographed, was on the water for just a few seconds then dissipated. The parent funnel was seen by hundreds of people across the area. No damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western and central Wisconsin on July 7th. The first round developed in the afternoon as a warm front was moving across the region. The second round moved in from Minnesota during the evening and was associated with a cold front. Both rounds of storms primarily produced large hail, but some sporadic wind damage also occurred. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Modena (Buffalo County). Trees and power lines were blown down in Osseo (Trempealeau County), Hatfield (Jackson County) and across rural Juneau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Germantown township in east central Juneau County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across western Wisconsin during the evening hours of June 28th ahead of a trough of low pressure. Several of these storms produced high winds that caused considerable damage in the city of La Crosse north into Holmen (La Crosse County). Numerous trees and power lines were blown down by winds that gusted to at least 60 mph. At least 7,000 customers in the La Crosse area were without power. The power outage took out street lights and stop lights across much of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Holmen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa. As the storms rolled across western Wisconsin they produced sporadic wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes in Platteville (Grant County) where 5 injuries occurred. The tornadoes caused extensive damage to the University of Wisconsin - Platteville campus and to numerous homes and business near the campus. Damage estimates to the campus alone were near $10 million. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for three southern Wisconsin counties, including Grant. The U.S. Small Business Administration approved a request for a disaster declaration in Grant County. The disaster declaration allows homeowners and businesses to obtain loans to repair or replace damaged or destroyed property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado (EF1) hit the northeast corner of Platteville, near the Golf and Country Club.  The track was about 1/2 mile long with a 50 yard width.  Damage included some trees knocked down and a roof off an apartment building.

Damage estimates to the campus alone were near $10 million. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for three southern Wisconsin counties, including Grant. The U.S. Small Business Administration approved a request for a disaster declaration in Grant County. The disaster declaration allows homeowners and businesses to obtain loans to repair or replace damaged or destroyed property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at the Goose Island Park and Campground just south of La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa. As the storms rolled across western Wisconsin they produced sporadic wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes in Platteville (Grant County) where 5 injuries occurred. The tornadoes caused extensive damage to the University of Wisconsin - Platteville campus and to numerous homes and business near the campus. Damage estimates to the campus alone were near $10 million. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for three southern Wisconsin counties, including Grant. The U.S. Small Business Administration approved a request for a disaster declaration in Grant County. The disaster declaration allows homeowners and businesses to obtain loans to repair or replace damaged or destroyed property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near the University of Wisconsin - La Crosse campus and in downtown La Crosse. One of the trees landed on a car in a UW - La Crosse parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of June 18th along a nearly stationary front. These storms produced very heavy rain that led to flash flooding occurring across portions of Trempealeau, Jackson and Monroe Counties. In addition, these storms also produced some strong winds that knocked down trees near West Salem (La Crosse County) and Adams (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused water to go over several roads across southern Trempealeau County including County Roads D and T and Oak Ridge Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing cold front over northeast Iowa during the evening of June 29th. As these storms moved across southwest Wisconsin they produced a tornado near Fennimore (Grant County) that produced considerable tree damage, destroyed several farm outbuildings and damaged three homes. The storms also produced damaging winds that blew over vehicles near Mt. Hope (Grant County) and Fennimore. Barns were blown down near Sinsinawa (Grant County) and Louisburg (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a house near Castle Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western and central Wisconsin on July 7th. The first round developed in the afternoon as a warm front was moving across the region. The second round moved in from Minnesota during the evening and was associated with a cold front. Both rounds of storms primarily produced large hail, but some sporadic wind damage also occurred. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Modena (Buffalo County). Trees and power lines were blown down in Osseo (Trempealeau County), Hatfield (Jackson County) and across rural Juneau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in a path from Leon to a couple miles southeast of Leon. The hail broke some lights on a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 7th. These storms became severe as they moved from central to east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. There was a brief tornado touchdown in southern Washington County at the St. Croix Bluffs Regional Park, and it moved eastward across the St. Croix River and caused minor damage to trees before lifting about a mile into Pierce County.

Heavy rain lead to flash flooding of small creeks in southern Sauk County.

After another Upper Midwest Blizzard, temperatures dropped into the teens below zero or lower, with winds averaging 10 to 20 mph from the northwest. This created wind chill values of 35 below zero for a long period from Sunday January 26th, to Tuesday, January 28th. Although there was a period where wind chill values rose above 35 below zero during the day of Monday, January, 27th, these values once again dropped to dangerous readings of 35 below zero by the evening. Many schools, businesses and recreational activities were closed.||Some of the lowest wind chill values include;||-45 degrees at Cumberland. |-43 degrees at Ladysmith. |-41 degrees at Eau Claire. |-41 degrees at New Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped below -35 degrees across the county and last for numerous hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front surged southward across the Upper Midwest early Wednesday morning January 22nd. After blizzard conditions subsided during the afternoon, skies cleared and temperatures dropped into the teens below zero. As winds continued around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values dropped to -35F Thursday morning, January 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to 35 below zero across the county during the morning of Thursday, January 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but potent low pressure system tracked along the South Dakota/Nebraska border late on January 29th and across southeast Minnesota on the morning of January 30th.  As it pushed through the area, it strengthened and produced a narrow band of heavy snow on its north side that bisected east central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin.  The heavy snow arrived a few hours before the morning commute began and peaked during the commute, producing snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times.  This led to severe societal impacts with hundreds of spinouts and commute times that doubled or tripled that of normal.  The warning had been issued due to heavy snowfall rates during the morning commute.  The snow quickly tapered off during the late morning hours, but areas of blowing snow persisted into the afternoon as northerly winds increased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snowfall observers measured 3 to 7 inches before the storm moved off to the east during the late morning. The city of Boyceville had the highest measured report of 7.3 inches.

The coldest wind chills in many years moved across the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest the week of January 5th, 2014. Temperatures dropped into the 20s and 30s below zero range and with wind speeds around 10 to 20 mph, wind chill values plummeted to 40 to 55 degrees below zero. The worst wind chill values occurred Sunday night (January 5) and Monday morning (January 6) when the core of the coldest air moved across Minnesota and Wisconsin.  A 76 year old woman walked away from an assisted living facility in Barron early on January 8 and succumbed to the cold that morning.  Wind chill warnings were in effect across the entire region.||The following are some of the lowest wind chill readings during the two day period. ||New Richmond, (St. Croix County), -50.                        |Cumberland, (Barron County), -50.                 |Ladysmith, (Rusk County), -49.                       |Stanley, (Chippewa County), -49.         |Menomonie, (Dunn County), -48.                     |Osceola, (Polk County), -48.                  |River Falls, (Pierce County), -46.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources from local observations indicated that a prolonged period of wind chill values below -35F occurred in the county, with locally -50F or lower during the height of the cold conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across the Upper Midwest the morning of Tuesday, January 14th. Initially this storm system was weak as it produced one to two inches of snow across portions of central and east central Minnesota prior to sunrise. After 6 am, the storm quickly intensified and produced snowfall rates of 1 to 1.5 inches per hour across far east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. By the early afternoon, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across west central Wisconsin from near Osceola to Chippewa Falls and Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches occurred across Polk County in a 10 hour period Tuesday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep low pressure system moved from the southern Rockies through Iowa and central Wisconsin, bringing widespread heavy snow to northwest Wisconsin. There was widespread snowfall of 8 to 15 across northwest Wisconsin, and some areas received even more.  Specific snowfall reports included 21 in Gile, 18 in Superior, 16.5 in Poplar, 16 in Seeley, 16 near Washburn, 13 in Ashland, 10 in Hertel, 9 near Springbrook, and 8.8 near Park Falls. ||The precipitation began as light rain for parts of northwest Wisconsin where temperatures warmed to near 40 degrees, but it was short-lived and quickly changed to snow during the late afternoon and evening of the 20th.  Strong winds developed in the wake of the storm, with gusts of about 30 to 40 mph. This caused major blowing and drifting snow, and low visibility.

A rapidly deepening low pressure area moved north through Wisconsin with strong west to northwest winds in its wake. The strong winds began with the passage of the occluded front during the evening of February 20th. The winds weakened by late afternoon or evening on February 21st. The high winds contributed to the cause of a multi-vehicle accident that involved one death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving weather system dropped 5 to 7 inches of snow in 12 hours across central and east central Wisconsin as it moved across the state.  The highest snowfall totals included 7.8 inches in Chilton (Calumet Co.), 7.5 inches in Ephraim (Door Co.), and 7.2 inches in Cleveland (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across Door County were mostly in the 5 to 7 inch range. The highest total was 7.5 inches at Ephraim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds developed as strong low pressure moved across northwest Wisconsin, and its trailing cold front moved across southern WI. Some areas had sustained winds in the 30 mph range with wind gusts in the 40 mph range. The only damage reported was a few large trees down in northern Iowa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa County Sheriff department reported a few large trees came down on the county roadways in the northern portion of the county. The Mineral Point and Lone Rock airports reported south to southwesterly winds sustained up to 30 mph with wind gusts to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from western Missouri into lower Michigan on February 17th. The band of heavy snow that fell on the northwest side of the low produced snowfall amounts around 6 inches across the central and eastern portions of Wisconsin. This band of heavy snow clipped Adams and Taylor Counties with 5 to 6 inches. The highest reported total was 6.2 inches in Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Adams County. The highest reported total was 6.2 inches in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving weather system dropped 5 to 7 inches of snow in 12 hours across central and east central Wisconsin as it moved across the state.  The highest snowfall totals included 7.8 inches in Chilton (Calumet Co.), 7.5 inches in Ephraim (Door Co.), and 7.2 inches in Cleveland (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in Manitowoc County were mostly in the 4 to 6 inch range.  The highest reported total was 7.2 inches at Cleveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening low pressure area moved north through Wisconsin with strong west to northwest winds in its wake. The strong winds began with the passage of the occluded front during the evening of February 20th. The winds weakened by late afternoon or evening on February 21st. The high winds contributed to the cause of a multi-vehicle accident that involved one death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two traffic lights fell over from the high winds in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest wind chills in many years moved across the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest the week of January 5th, 2014. The worst wind chill values occurred Sunday night (January 5) and Monday morning (January 6) when the core of the coldest air moved across Minnesota and Wisconsin. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep low pressure system moved from the southern Rockies through Iowa and central Wisconsin, bringing widespread heavy snow to northwest Wisconsin. There was widespread snowfall of 8 to 15 across northwest Wisconsin, and some areas received even more.  Specific snowfall reports included 21 in Gile, 18 in Superior, 16.5 in Poplar, 16 in Seeley, 16 near Washburn, 13 in Ashland, 10 in Hertel, 9 near Springbrook, and 8.8 near Park Falls. ||The precipitation began as light rain for parts of northwest Wisconsin where temperatures warmed to near 40 degrees, but it was short-lived and quickly changed to snow during the late afternoon and evening of the 20th.  Strong winds developed in the wake of the storm, with gusts of about 30 to 40 mph. This caused major blowing and drifting snow, and low visibility.

A bitterly cold arctic air mass, the coldest to impact the region in years, spread across the area following the passage of a cold front.  Temperatures fell to lows in the 16 below to 32 below zero range.  The cold temperatures, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills in the 40 below to 55 below zero range.  The coldest temperatures and wind chills were generally across central and north central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded temperature in Brown County was 18 degrees below zero at Denmark. Wind chills were as low as 45 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest temperature recorded in Brown County was 16 degrees below zero at Denmark. Wind chills were as low as 36 below zero.

Parts of western Wisconsin were hit by heavy snow on February 20th and 21st as an area of low pressure tracked from southern Kansas to Lake Superior. This storm system produced almost a foot of heavy, wet snow that was then blown around by winds gusting as high as 40 mph. The drifting snow created by the winds created very poor driving conditions and closed some roads. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, created considerable blowing and drifting snow across Buffalo County after 5 inches of snow fell. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions. Most of the schools across the county dismissed early on the 20th and then were either closed or delayed the start of classes on the 21st. The highest reported snowfall amount was 5 inches in Alma.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to 35 below zero across the county during the morning of Thursday, January 23rd. One of the lowest wind chill value was -39 degrees that occurred at the Cumberland airport.

A small, but potent low pressure system tracked along the South Dakota/Nebraska border late on January 29th and across southeast Minnesota on the morning of January 30th.  As it pushed through the area, it strengthened and produced a narrow band of heavy snow on its north side that bisected east central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin.  The heavy snow arrived a few hours before the morning commute began and peaked during the commute, producing snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times.  This led to severe societal impacts with hundreds of spinouts and commute times that doubled or tripled that of normal.  The warning had been issued due to heavy snowfall rates during the morning commute.  The snow quickly tapered off during the late morning hours, but areas of blowing snow persisted into the afternoon as northerly winds increased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snowfall observers measured 3 to 6 inches before the storm moved off to the east during the late morning. The city of Rice Lake had the highest measured report of 6.2 inches.

Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western and central Wisconsin on July 7th. The first round developed in the afternoon as a warm front was moving across the region. The second round moved in from Minnesota during the evening and was associated with a cold front. Both rounds of storms primarily produced large hail, but some sporadic wind damage also occurred. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Modena (Buffalo County). Trees and power lines were blown down in Osseo (Trempealeau County), Hatfield (Jackson County) and across rural Juneau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail fell in Sparta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front had stalled over southern Wisconsin with repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. There was some flash flooding in southeast Wisconsin.

A Quasi-linear convective system with a cyclonic rotating comma head on the north end produced numerous tornadoes and damaging straight line winds. Governor Walker declared a State of Emergency for Green and Dane counties due to the extensive damage from severe thunderstorms and tornadoes that occurred from June 16-18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large poll shed was mostly destroyed by straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving and training thunderstorms produce areas of very heavy rain and flash flooding over portions of south central WI. A portion of Madison experienced 4-6 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets are flooded in the city of Beloit with rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing cold front over northeast Iowa during the evening of June 29th. As these storms moved across southwest Wisconsin they produced a tornado near Fennimore (Grant County) that produced considerable tree damage, destroyed several farm outbuildings and damaged three homes. The storms also produced damaging winds that blew over vehicles near Mt. Hope (Grant County) and Fennimore. Barns were blown down near Sinsinawa (Grant County) and Louisburg (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 2 miles northeast of Fennimore and skipped along an east path to 3 miles northwest of Montfort.  The tornado damaged several mature hardwood trees, several outbuildings and three homes. On one farm alone, eight buildings were destroyed. The homes sustained minor damage compared to the hardwood trees and outbuildings.  The tornado was rated an EF1 with maximum estimated winds of 110 mph.  Total path length was 8.2 miles and the maximum width of the tornado was 600 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms raced across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours producing straight line wind damage and one brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down. Blown transformers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale bow echo raced east to northeast across southern WI, causing straight line wind damage in many areas. We Energies reported a maximum power outage of 110000 homes which has not happened since 2005. The outages were due to straight line winds and lightning impacting the power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen inch diameter tree knocked down onto highway 145.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northwest Wisconsin, on Monday (July 21st). A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin. Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex. This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and then northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd). This derecho brought winds of at least 50 to 70 mph to parts of northwest Wisconsin, which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb fell onto a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, August 18th across Wisconsin and Minnesota. Two of the storms became severe near Menomonie and Eau Claire.
A warm front had stalled over southern Wisconsin with repeated rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. There was some flash flooding in southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain causing minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed ahead of an advancing cold front produced large hail across parts of western Wisconsin during the afternoon of May 19th. The largest hail reported was quarter sized near Patch Grove (Grant County) and Osseo (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Neillsville, 2.25 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed ahead of an advancing cold front produced large hail across parts of western Wisconsin during the afternoon of May 19th. The largest hail reported was quarter sized near Patch Grove (Grant County) and Osseo (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from locally heavy rain pushed the Black River out of its banks in Black River Falls. The river crested almost 3 feet over the flood stage at 49.86 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on April 12th. These storms developed along a very slow moving cold front and primarily produced large hail, although some occasional high winds were also reported. The first round of storms moved in during the mid morning hours with the last of the storms ending during the early evening. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized near Stoddard (Vernon County) and near Viroqua (Vernon County).

A shortwave trough and the associated surface trough triggered clusters of showers and thunderstorms over southern Wisconsin during the evening. A thunderstorm became rooted to a pseudo lake breeze/outflow boundary moving inland from Lake Michigan. The thunderstorm developed supercell characteristics and dropped a downburst on the west side of Waukesha. The thunderstorm them morphed into a small segment squall line   with damaging winds on the leading gust front as it moved through Kenosha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst produced a sporadic north to south path of wind damage on the west side of Waukesha. Several large trees were knocked down with one tree partially uprooted. Some shingles were removed from home roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 7th. These storms became severe as they moved from central to east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. There was a brief tornado touchdown in southern Washington County at the St. Croix Bluffs Regional Park, and it moved eastward across the St. Croix River and caused minor damage to trees before lifting about a mile into Pierce County.

A severe thunderstorm developed during the afternoon across central Minnesota, moved east-southeast across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the evening hours. Large hail developed near McKinley, and moved southeast across Cumberland before weakening near Rice Lake. Baseball size hail was reported north and northeast of McKinley, along with a few downed trees.

Thunderstorms formed in a warm and humid airmass.  Isolated wet microbursts from the storms produced winds of 55 to 60 mph that snapped and uprooted trees in eastern Waupaca County, southern Oconto County and southwest Winnebago County.  Heavy rainfall caused street flooding in Outagamie County, southern Winnebago County and southern Calumet County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms caused street flooding in Black Creek, at the intersection Main Street and Oak Street.

Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed and moved east to northeast across southern Wisconsin. Dozens of hail reports were received with only a couple wind damage reports. Heavy rain lead to flash flooding of small creeks in southern Sauk County.
A line of severe thunderstorms raced across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours producing straight line wind damage and one brief tornado.

Two rounds of thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary front during the early morning hours of August 25th across western Wisconsin. The first round of storms blew down some trees near Hixton (Jackson County) and hit Granton (Clark County) with estimated winds of 70 mph. The second round of storms blew down trees from Brice Prairie (La Crosse County) through Melrose (Jackson County) to near Black River Falls (Jackson County). Several trees and a barn were blow down near Cataract (Monroe County) and a business in Sparta (Monroe County) had shingles removed from the roof by the high winds. Some hail also fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on County Road S in Onalaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 1st along a trough of low pressure.
